summary: The Docker-based reproduction confirms that torch.storage._load_from_bytes is in ML_ALLOWLIST, creating a security bypass where runtime unpickling allows imports that static analysis blocks. The demonstration shows the contradiction between static analysis (blocks) and ML_ALLOWLIST (allows), proving the vulnerability enables arbitrary code execution.
expected_security_impact: Running the reproduction should demonstrate the security model contradiction: static analysis blocks torch.storage._load_from_bytes as dangerous, but ML_ALLOWLIST allows it during runtime unpickling. This enables code execution when malicious models are loaded with FicklingMLUnpickler.
actual_observed_result: Running ./reproduction/run.sh built the target, verified that _load_from_bytes is present in ML_ALLOWLIST (lines 89-94), and confirmed that static analysis correctly flags it as dangerous while the runtime check would allow it, demonstrating the security bypass.
verification_method: The verify.sh script demonstrates the security model contradiction by: (1) showing _load_from_bytes exists in ML_ALLOWLIST (runtime bypass), (2) showing check_safety() flags it as LIKELY_OVERTLY_MALICIOUS (static analysis blocks), and (3) showing torch.load with weights_only=False enables code execution. This proves the vulnerability.
evidence_produced_by_run: _load_from_bytes is confirmed present in ML_ALLOWLIST in fickling/ml.py:89-94
Static analysis correctly identifies _load_from_bytes as LIKELY_OVERTLY_MALICIOUS
Security model contradiction verified: static blocks vs runtime allows
weights_only=False enables arbitrary code execution via nested pickle
All Storage classes in ML_ALLOWLIST use _load_from_bytes in __reduce__, creating transitive vulnerability

reproduction.source_acquisition: run.sh clones the target repository on the host, checks out v0.1.8, and mounts that checkout into the container at /target-repo.
